answers.field_label: Title Of The Bachelor Or Master Thesis
answers.value: Digitalisation and Resilience of Small and Medium-Sized Enterprises in South Tyrol: Evidence from the Tourism and Craft Sectors
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.value:
1. Background and motivation

South Tyrol’s economy is characterised by a dense network of small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs), particularly in tourism, crafts, and agriculture. Over the last decade, and especially since the COVID-19 pandemic, digitalisation has moved from being a competitive advantage to an essential condition for survival and long-term resilience. However, many South Tyrolean SMEs still face structural challenges: limited internal expertise, language diversity (German, Italian, and Ladin), seasonality of demand (especially in tourism), and strong family-business traditions that can both support and hinder innovation.

I grew up in a small guesthouse family business in the Puster Valley, so I have personally observed how difficult it can be for small companies to evaluate digital tools, invest in them, and then integrate them into everyday work. During the pandemic, my parents struggled with online booking platforms, digital guest communication, and new hygiene and tracing regulations that were often communicated digitally. At the same time, some neighbouring businesses that had previously invested in digital processes (e.g., online check-in, digital vouchers, social media marketing) recovered more quickly and even expanded their customer base.

This personal experience, combined with my academic interest in regional economics and innovation, motivates me to investigate how digitalisation concretely contributes to the resilience of South Tyrolean SMEs, focusing on two key sectors: tourism (e.g., hotels, guesthouses, tour operators) and crafts (e.g., carpenters, electricians, small manufacturing workshops). I want to understand not only which digital tools are used, but also how they are implemented, how they change internal processes, and which factors explain differences between firms that successfully digitalise and those that lag behind.

2. Research objectives and questions

The overarching objective of the thesis is to analyse the relationship between the degree and type of digitalisation and the resilience of South Tyrolean SMEs in the tourism and craft sectors.

The thesis will address the following main research questions:

1. What is the current state of digitalisation in a selected sample of South Tyrolean SMEs in tourism and crafts (e.g., use of online booking systems, digital payment solutions, ERP systems, social media, e-commerce, digital communication tools)?
2. How do SME owners and managers perceive the impact of digital tools on their ability to cope with crises and external shocks (e.g., pandemic, energy price increases, labour shortages) and on their long-term competitiveness?
3. Which internal and external factors (e.g., firm size, generational structure, education level of owner/manager, support from local institutions, availability of digital skills in the region) facilitate or hinder successful digitalisation in these sectors?
4. Are there systematic differences between tourism and craft SMEs in terms of digitalisation strategies, barriers, and outcomes, and what can each sector learn from the other?

3. Theoretical framework

The thesis will be grounded in three main strands of literature:

a) SME digitalisation: I will review existing research on how small and medium-sized enterprises approach digital transformation, with particular attention to family businesses, rural regions, and Alpine contexts. This includes the distinction between basic digitalisation (e.g., having a website, email, and simple online booking) and more advanced digitalisation (e.g., integrated CRM systems, data analytics, platform-based business models).

b) Regional and resilience economics: I will use concepts from regional economics and resilience theory to define and operationalise "resilience" at the firm level (e.g., ability to maintain employment, quickly adapt business models, or diversify markets in response to shocks). I will also consider how regional characteristics of South Tyrol (bilingualism/trilingualism, cross-border location, strong tourism, and highly developed craft sector) influence both digitalisation and resilience.

c) Innovation and capability-based perspectives: I will draw on the resource-based view and dynamic capabilities theory to understand how internal capabilities (e.g., digital skills, learning culture, openness to innovation) shape the adoption and effective use of digital technologies in SMEs.

4. Methodology and data

The thesis will adopt a mixed-methods approach, combining quantitative and qualitative data collection and analysis.

4.1. Quantitative component

I will design an online questionnaire targeting owners and managers of SMEs in the tourism and craft sectors in South Tyrol. The questionnaire will be made available in German and Italian to maximise participation and reflect the linguistic reality of the region.

The survey will collect data on:
- Basic firm characteristics: sector, size (employees, turnover range), location (valley, town, altitude), ownership structure (family vs. non-family), generational stage (first, second, third generation, etc.).
- Digitalisation level: presence of website, online booking or ordering systems, use of social media channels, digital payment options, internal software (accounting, ERP, CRM), cloud services, remote work tools, etc.
- Perceived impact of digital tools: Likert-scale items on how digitalisation has affected efficiency, customer satisfaction, revenue stability, ability to reach new markets, and flexibility during crises.
- Barriers and enablers: questions on financial constraints, lack of skills, time constraints, trust in digital providers, support from local associations and the Chamber of Commerce, and participation in training programmes.
- Resilience indicators: self-assessed ability to cope with recent shocks (e.g., COVID-19, energy price increases), changes in employment, turnover volatility, and speed of recovery.

I aim for a sample of approximately 80–120 SMEs, balancing tourism and crafts as far as possible. I will seek support from the Chamber of Commerce of Bolzano and local associations (e.g., HGV for tourism enterprises and LVH for crafts) to distribute the survey link via newsletters or mailing lists.

The quantitative data will be analysed using descriptive statistics (to map the current state of digitalisation) and regression or correlation analysis (to examine the relationship between digitalisation indicators and resilience outcomes, controlling for firm size, sector, and other relevant variables). Where possible, I will compare subgroups (e.g., family vs. non-family firms, valley vs. city locations) to identify patterns.

4.2. Qualitative component

To deepen the understanding of processes and perceptions behind the numbers, I will conduct 10–15 semi-structured interviews with selected SME owners and managers from both sectors and with different levels of digitalisation (from very traditional to highly digitalised companies).

The interviews will focus on:
- The history of the company and previous experiences with crises (e.g., financial crisis, COVID-19, climate-related events).
- The decision-making process behind adopting (or not adopting) specific digital tools.
- Concrete examples of how digital tools helped or did not help during specific challenging periods (e.g., lockdowns, staff shortages, supply chain issues).
- The role of younger family members or external consultants in driving digital change.
- Experiences with public support programmes, training courses, and the role of local institutions (including the Chamber of Commerce) in supporting digitalisation.

I will record (with consent), transcribe, and code the interviews using qualitative content analysis, identifying recurring themes, success factors, and typical obstacles.

5. Expected contribution and relevance for the South Tyrolean economy

The thesis aims to provide both academic and practical contributions:

- For academic research, it will offer an empirically grounded case study of digitalisation and resilience in a specific Alpine region with a strong SME structure. By combining quantitative and qualitative data, it will help refine theoretical understandings of how digital capabilities translate into resilient behaviour in small, often family-run businesses.

- For practitioners and policymakers in South Tyrol, including the Chamber of Commerce, the results will provide a differentiated picture of where SMEs stand in their digital transformation and which concrete measures could support them. For example, the study may highlight the need for more targeted training programmes in German and Italian, sector-specific digitalisation guides, or mentoring schemes that connect more advanced firms with those that are just starting their digital journey.

I intend to summarise the most practice-oriented findings in a short German- and Italian-language executive summary that could be shared with participating companies and relevant institutions.

6. Feasibility and timetable

The project is planned as a Master’s thesis with a total workload of 30 ECTS. I have already discussed the topic with my supervisor, Prof. Dr. Marta Rossi, who has experience with similar empirical projects in regional economics.

The preliminary timetable is as follows:
- March–April 2026: Detailed literature review on SME digitalisation, resilience, and regional economic development; refinement of research questions and conceptual framework.
- May 2026: Design and pre-testing of the questionnaire and interview guide; ethical review and data protection considerations; coordination with the Chamber of Commerce and local associations for survey distribution.
- June–July 2026: Data collection (online survey) and initial processing; selection of interview partners.
- August–September 2026: Conducting and transcribing interviews; quantitative data analysis (descriptive statistics and regression models).
- October–November 2026: Qualitative content analysis; integration of quantitative and qualitative findings; drafting of thesis chapters.
- December 2026–January 2027: Revision, proofreading, and final submission of the thesis.

7. Personal qualification

Through my studies in Entrepreneurship and Innovation at the Free University of Bozen-Bolzano, I have completed courses in quantitative methods, regional economics, SME management, and digital business models. I have already worked on smaller empirical projects using survey tools (Qualtrics) and statistical software (SPSS and basic R). I also work part-time in my family’s guesthouse, where I help manage our online presence (website updates, booking.com and Airbnb listings, and social media posts), which gives me practical insight into digital challenges and opportunities in the tourism sector.

My language skills (German as mother tongue, Italian at C1 level, and English at C1 level) will help me to communicate effectively with a broad range of South Tyrolean entrepreneurs and to access international academic literature.

In summary, the thesis will investigate how digitalisation shapes the resilience of South Tyrolean SMEs in tourism and crafts, using a solid empirical approach and with clear practical relevance for the region’s economic development.
